parameters.name: Scheduled sleep per crewmember per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (Langston 2005, ISS)
parameters.notes: Flight actuals: astronauts sleep only ~6.09 h/night on ISS (Barger et al. 2014, Lancet Neurol 13:904). Schedule 8.5 h; model the deficit as a performance risk, not recoverable labor. Scale by 1.0275 for per-sol budgets. An additional invariant 1.5 h/CM-day of pre-sleep (1.0) + post-sleep (0.5) time, including personal hygiene, brackets the sleep period (BVAD Table 3-28).

parameters.name: Scheduled work (systems+utilization+maintenance ops) per workday
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28; ISS standard workweek
parameters.notes: 5 workdays + 2 weekend days/week (weekend has only ~0.3 h/d payload tending); 8 vacation days/yr. Mars surface work rate needs +0.7 h/CM-d vs ISS in NASA integrated model (Stromgren 2015).

parameters.name: Exercise incl. setup, stow, hygiene per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28
parameters.notes: Device time ~1.5 h (aerobic+resistive), rest is overhead. BVAD assumes -0.5 h/d in hypogravity (surface); transit may require more. 7 days/week including rest days.

parameters.name: Meals (prep+consumption+cleanup) per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (1.0 h per meal x 3)
parameters.notes: Prepackaged food system. Does not diminish on rest days. Preparation component broken out separately in hf.time.food_prep_* parameters.

parameters.name: Planning/reporting/work-prep overhead per workday
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (DPC 0.5 + plan review/report 1.0 + work prep 0.5)
parameters.notes: Mars autonomy (4-24 min one-way light time) shifts this from ground-conference to onboard planning; NASA Mars crew time model increased training/work-prep liens for autonomous ops.

parameters.name: Housekeeping and laundry per week
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (2.0 h on each of 2 weekend days)
parameters.notes: ISS actual scheduling. Larger habitats with dust ingress (Mars EVA ops) plausibly need more; upper range is an engineering allowance.

parameters.name: Recreation/off-duty block per weekend day (psychological constraint)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28
parameters.notes: Treat as a hard scheduling constraint (2 contiguous rest days/week) for behavioral health; includes private family/psych conferences ~1 h/wk. Not harvestable labor except in short (<28 d) surge periods.

parameters.name: Variably-Scheduled Time (taxable labor pool) per week, planetary surface
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-29 and sect. 3.2.5
parameters.notes: Includes work, meals, housekeeping, LSS ops. 67.2 h/wk in weightlessness (transit). Max (76.7) sustainable <=28 days per Skylab. Min 50 = minimum expected work output. Per sol multiply daily value by 1.0275.

parameters.name: Food preparation labor, fully prepackaged food system
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), sect. 4.5.3 (Shuttle experience 45-90 min/d for crew of 6)
parameters.notes: Subset of the 3.0 h/d meals block. Use for resupplied-food scenarios.

parameters.name: Food preparation labor, fully crop-based (bioregenerative) diet
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), sect. 4.5.3 (Hunter model; LMLSTP Phase III measured 4.6 CM-h/d for crew of 4)
parameters.notes: EXCLUDES crop processing into ingredients (unquantified in BVAD) and crop tending (belongs to food/plant domain). 50/50 mixed diet = 0.50 h/CM-day. Major labor lever for settlement-scale food closure.

parameters.name: ECLSS preventive+corrective upkeep per habitat per day (2-3 crew scale)
parameters.source: Russell & Klaus 2007, Reliab. Eng. Syst. Saf. 92:808-820 (ISS/Mir-derived)
parameters.notes: Per habitat, not per crewmember; scales sub-linearly with crew. Baseline for ISS-class ECLSS technology; regenerative closure increases it.

parameters.name: Corrective (unscheduled) maintenance, expected value
parameters.source: Stromgren et al. 2021, IEEE Aerospace, NTRS 20210026843 (lunar Surface Habitat, ISS component data: expected 24.7 h per 28-day 2-crew mission; 50th pct 9.0 h, 99th pct 70.5 h)
parameters.notes: Right-skewed (lognormal-like): range_min=50th pct, range_max=99th pct. ECLSS dominates. Scales with installed equipment count, not crew size; renormalize per habitat for settlement scale.

parameters.name: Maximum EVA time per crewmember per week
parameters.source: NASA HAT EVA team constraint, in Stromgren et al. 2015, NTRS 20150009469
parameters.notes: With one full rest day per 7 days. NASA model also imposes 30-day post-landing reconditioning before first EVA (long-stay). BVAD lunar outpost: 80 CM-h/wk total EVA at 7 two-person sorties/wk.

parameters.name: ISS EVA total prep + post crew-hours per EVA (actuals, microgravity EMU)
parameters.source: NASA EVA-EXP-0031 figures cited in Griffin, AIAA 2020-4170 (58.25 h prep + 15.83 h post, typically 2-person EVA)
parameters.notes: Total crew-hours across EV+IV crew over several days: procedures, tool config, suit servicing, prebreathe. Work efficiency index 0.39-0.51. Upper bound / legacy-ops anchor for Mars EVA overhead trades. ISS gas cost: ~9.1 kg prebreathe O2 + 1.8 kg airlock air lost per EVA.

parameters.name: Mars surface EVA overhead per crewmember per sortie (design case)
parameters.source: Estimate: exploration atmosphere 56.5 kPa/34% O2 gives ~15-min prebreathe (Abercromby et al. 2015, Acta Astronautica 109:76-87) + don/doff, checkout, dust mitigation, amortized suit servicing
parameters.notes: No flight data for planetary suits since Apollo; sensitivity-test between this and ISS actuals. Assumes suitport or high-cadence airlock ops.

parameters.name: EVA oxygen usage + leakage per suited hour
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 4-69 (lunar surface EVA values)
parameters.notes: xEMU suitport scenario: 0.10 kg/CM-h + 0.54 kg/CM-EVA fixed (N2 purge dominated). Metabolic rate suited: 295-352 W (Apollo surface avg ~1030 kJ/h).

parameters.name: EVA cooling water (sublimator/evaporator) loss per suited hour
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 4-69 (polar 0.25-0.5; equatorial 0.46-0.76)
parameters.notes: Thermal-environment dependent; lunar-derived. Mars ambient (colder, CO2 atmosphere) may allow radiator-augmented PLSS with lower water use; treat as upper-bound consumable.

parameters.name: Minimum acceptable Net Habitable Volume per crewmember, long-duration
parameters.source: Whitmire et al. 2015, NASA/TM-2015-218564 (SME consensus, NTRS 20140016951); consistent with NASA-STD-3001 Vol 2 task-based NHV requirement
parameters.notes: Floor for ~1-2.5 yr missions with countermeasures in place; permanent settlement plausibly needs more (no data; range_max is engineering allowance). Crew of 6 -> 150 m3 NHV minimum.

parameters.name: Private crew quarters volume per crewmember
parameters.source: Whitmire et al. 2015 consensus report (5.4 m3 allocation); ISS USOS CQ flight actual 2.1 m3 (Broyan et al., 08ICES-0222)
parameters.notes: 2.1 m3 (ISS) is the demonstrated floor and is considered marginal for multi-year missions; 5.4 m3 recommended for long-duration. Include radiation-augmented quarters if used as SPE shelter.

parameters.name: Mars surface GCR dose equivalent rate (Gale crater, solar max era)
parameters.source: Hassler et al. 2014, Science 343:1244797 (MSL RAD); solar-cycle range per Guo et al. 2021, Astron. Astrophys. Rev. 29:8
parameters.notes: +/-0.12 measurement uncertainty; = 0.66 mSv/sol = ~233 mSv/Earth-yr. Absorbed dose 0.21 mGy/d. Higher at solar min and at higher elevation (thinner atmosphere). Thin-detector point dose equivalent; ICRP effective dose ~30% lower (Martinez-Sierra 2024). verified correction: the 'Martinez-Sierra 2024' attribution for the ICRP-effective-dose reduction is a phantom author for arXiv:2409.02001 (actually Ralha et al. 2024).

parameters.name: Interplanetary transit GCR dose equivalent rate (light shielding, solar max)
parameters.source: Zeitlin et al. 2013, Science 340:1080-1084 (MSL RAD cruise, 2011-12)
parameters.notes: +/-0.33 uncertainty; measured at solar max behind ~10-40 g/cm2 nonuniform shielding. Solar-min GCR can be ~50-60% higher. Round-trip fast-transit (~360 d) GCR alone ~0.66 Sv. SPE contributions additional and stochastic.

parameters.name: NASA career effective dose limit
parameters.source: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B (2022), per 2021 NAS review; universal all ages/sexes, 3% mean REID basis
parameters.notes: Binding constraint: an unmitigated conjunction mission (~360 d transit + 500 sols surface) accrues ~1.0 Sv. Dose tracker should flag crew approaching limit and drive shielding/timing trades. A settlement sim may need its own (non-NASA) risk policy for permanent residents.

parameters.name: Short-term (30-day) blood-forming-organ dose limit
parameters.unit: mSv NASA effective dose per design-reference SPE (Oct 1989 spectrum)
parameters.source: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B [V1 4031], per OCHMO-TB-020 Rev E (12/2022); legacy 30-day BFO 250 mGy-Eq limit is NCRP-132/pre-Rev-B and Townsend et al. 2018
parameters.notes: Deterministic (acute) effects limit; the design driver for SPE storm shelters, separate from the 600 mSv career (cancer) limit. verified correction: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B (2022) does NOT contain a 30-day BFO 250 mGy-Eq limit; requirement [V1 4031] is <250 mSv NASA effective dose per design-reference SPE (Oct 1989 spectrum). The 30-day BFO 250 / 1-yr 500 mGy-Eq limits are the retired NCRP-132/pre-Rev-B values (still used in shelter-design literature). A rolling-30-day-BFO tracker would enforce a retired requirement.

parameters.name: Mean vertical atmospheric shielding column at datum
parameters.unit: g/cm2 (mean vertical column at low-elevation sites, Gale-like, -4.4 km MOLA; ~16 g/cm2 at MOLA datum)
parameters.source: Guo et al. 2021, Astron. Astrophys. Rev. 29:8; Ralha et al. 2024, arXiv:2409.02001
parameters.notes: Slant-path average shielding over the sky is substantially higher than vertical. Removes most SPE protons <150 MeV: SPE shelters are primarily a transit problem. Seasonal pressure cycle modulates surface dose by a few percent. verified correction: arXiv:2409.02001 is Ralha et al. 2024 (no author 'Martinez-Sierra'); 23 g/cm2 is the mean column at Gale/low-elevation sites (-4.4 km MOLA), not the datum. At the MOLA datum mean column is ~16 g/cm2 - relabel to avoid double-counting elevation scaling (~2%/(g/cm2) dose sensitivity).

parameters.name: Dry-regolith depth below which shielding is counterproductive (neutron buildup)
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020, JGR Planets 125:e2019JE006246 (GEANT4/AtRIS, RAD-validated): equivalent dose peaks at 30-40 cm (88-102 g/cm2) and only drops below surface value beyond ~200 g/cm2 (0.7-0.9 m dry rock)
parameters.notes: Model-critical: <0.7 m of dry regolith cover INCREASES crew equivalent dose (up to ~+10-25% at the ~30 cm peak). Buildup is much weaker for hydrogen-rich (icy) regolith. range_min = buildup peak location.

parameters.name: Dry regolith depth to roughly halve surface GCR equivalent dose
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020, Table 3: 1.26 m andesite (rho=2.8 g/cm3, ~350 g/cm2) reduces water-sphere equivalent dose to 200 mSv/yr, <=half the dry-surface value; sandstone 1.64 m
parameters.notes: In water-sphere equivalent-dose metric at medium solar modulation (Phi=580 MV). Loose regolith (rho~1.5-1.7) needs proportionally more thickness for equal areal density. Hydrated regolith halves dose in substantially less depth.

parameters.name: Regolith depth to bring annual equivalent dose under 100 mSv/yr
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020 (0.8-2.5 m depending on composition; hydrated less); Zhang et al. 2022 JGR Planets: 1-1.6 m at representative sites
parameters.notes: 100 mSv/yr threshold chosen as the level above which lifetime cancer risk increase is evident. Water-ice-rich regolith (10-50 wt%) is the most mass-efficient cover; 10% subsurface water content alone cuts above-ground equivalent dose ~36%.

parameters.name: Dedicated SPE storm-shelter shielding (transit; aluminum-equivalent)
parameters.source: Townsend et al. 2018, Life Sci. Space Res. 17:32-39 (PMID 29753411); Kim et al. 2006: 5 g/cm2 Al insufficient for BFO in Aug-1972-class event; ~10 g/cm2 meets 30-day BFO limits; 20+ g/cm2 (hydrogenous) for extreme events
parameters.notes: Water/polyethylene ~1.5-2x more effective than Al per g/cm2. Occupancy 12-36 h/event (all crew), warning minutes-hours: costs crew time and interrupts EVA. On surface, any >=200 g/cm2 regolith-covered module trivially satisfies SPE sheltering.

parameters.name: Clinical medical events (all severities), long-duration spaceflight
parameters.source: Crucian et al. 2016, Int. J. Gen. Med. 9:383-391 (ISS incidence; skin rash 1.12/py, upper-respiratory 0.97/py leading; infections ~0.6/py)
parameters.notes: Mostly minor/self-limited. Model as Poisson with per-category labor cost (patient downtime hours-days + caregiver ~0.5-2 h/d for significant events). ISS cohort is screened/healthy; settlement demographics would raise rates.

parameters.name: Evacuation-class medical events
parameters.source: Derived estimate from Antonsen et al. 2022 Fig. 4 (EVAC 0.3-1% per <42-d mission, 4 crew, unlimited ISS capability); not a stated IMM rate
parameters.notes: No actual ISS medical evacuation has occurred. For Mars (no evac possible): treat as severe-event rate requiring definitive onboard care; IMM projects >10% probability of an evac-class condition on a 730+ day mission even with ISS-level capability, and ~0.01 floor on medical loss-of-crew-life per 1195-d, 4-crew mission. verified correction: Antonsen et al. 2022 reports mission-level IMM probabilities (EVAC 0.3-1% for <42-day missions, >10% for 730+ day missions), not a 0.017 events/person-year rate; the value is derivable from those figures but should be labeled a derived estimate, not a published IMM rate.

modeling_notes: LABOR SUPPLY (per CM-sol, sol = 24.6597 h; ISS 24.0-h budgets scaled by 1.0275): Invariant time (sleep 8.5 + pre/post-sleep 1.5 + exercise 2.5) ~12.8 h/sol; on workdays add planning 2.0 and meals 3.0, leaving ~6.7-6.9 h/sol of assignable work inside the 6.5-h-equivalent ops block; weekly liens: housekeeping 4 h/CM-wk, food prep 0.17 (packaged) to 0.83+ (grown) h/CM-sol, ECLSS upkeep 3.0-3.3 h/habitat-day, corrective maintenance stochastic (mean 0.44, 99th pct 1.26 h/CM-sol, lognormal, scales with installed equipment). Schedule 5 workdays + 2 rest days per 7-sol week (rest days are a behavioral-health constraint; surge >VST max 76.7 h/wk sustainable <=28 d). EVA: cap 24 h/CM/wk with 1 full rest day; charge per-CM overhead (1.5 h design / up to ISS-actual 74 crew-h per EVA for sensitivity), O2 0.092 kg/CM-h, cooling water 0.25-0.76 kg/CM-h, suitport fixed 0.54 kg O2/CM-EVA; 30-sol post-landing reconditioning with no EVA. Cross-check: NASA's integrated Mars model yields only ~8.5% of gross surface crew-hours as science/utilization - if your sim gives >>15%, liens are being dropped. RADIATION TRACKER: L0 dose_mSv = 1.84*days_transit + 0.66*S*sols_surface, S = shielding transfer factor; track vs 600 mSv career and 250 mGy-Eq/30-day BFO. L1: modulate GCR +/-20% with 11-yr solar cycle (anti-correlated with sunspot number), atmospheric column correction around 23 g/cm2, S(depth) lookup MUST reproduce the dry-regolith neutron buildup hump (S rises to ~1.1-1.25 at ~90 g/cm2, crosses 1.0 at ~200 g/cm2, ~0.5 at ~350 g/cm2, <=0.3 beyond ~2 m); SPEs as Poisson events (lambda ~2-6/yr solar max, ~0.2-1/yr min) mostly negligible on surface behind atmosphere but requiring 12-36 h shelter occupancy (labor hit) and dominating transit acute risk. L2: BON2020/DLR GCR spectra + HZETRN/GEANT4 response matrices through time-varying column density (diurnal/seasonal pressure from MCD, ~5% sub-sol dose modulation), ICRP effective dose on phantoms, validated against RAD 0.64 mSv/d at Gale (Phi~580 MV). METRIC CONSISTENCY: RAD point dose-equivalent, Rostel water-sphere equivalent dose, and ICRP effective dose differ +/-30-50%; pick one (recommend effective dose) and convert sources consistently. MEDICAL: Poisson 3.4 minor + 0.017 evac-class events/person-yr with labor costs; no-evac reality means severe events convert to onboard care demand and possible crew loss (~0.01/mission floor).
key_findings: ISS crew time budget (BVAD Table 3-28) is fully quantified and transfers to Mars with a 1.0275 sol scaling: 8.5 h sleep, 6.5 h scheduled work (5 d/wk), 2.5 h exercise, 3.0 h meals, 2.0 h planning overhead, 4 h/wk housekeeping; the taxable labor pool (VST) is 69.7 h/CM-week on a planetary surface (range 50-76.7).

After all liens, NASA's own integrated Mars crew-time model leaves only ~8.5% of gross surface crew-hours for science/utilization on a 460-sol stay - overhead, not science, dominates the human time economy.

Corrective maintenance is stochastic and right-skewed: expected ~0.44 h/CM-sol for an ISS-technology surface habitat but 99th percentile ~1.26 h/CM-sol (Stromgren 2021); ECLSS upkeep adds ~3.0-3.3 h/habitat-day (Russell & Klaus 2007).

EVA overhead spans two orders of magnitude between ISS actuals (~74 crew-h prep+post per EVA) and planetary design targets (~1.5 h/CM with the 56.5 kPa/34% O2 exploration atmosphere and 15-min prebreathe) - the single largest unproven human-factors assumption for Mars surface productivity.

Radiation is a binding design constraint: measured doses (0.64 mSv/day surface, Hassler 2014; 1.84 mSv/day transit, Zeitlin 2013) put an unmitigated conjunction mission at ~1.0 Sv versus NASA's 600 mSv career limit (NASA-STD-3001, 2021 standard).

Regolith shielding is non-monotonic: less than ~0.7-0.9 m (200 g/cm2) of dry regolith INCREASES equivalent dose via secondary neutrons; ~1.3 m dry rock halves it; 0.8-2.5 m brings it under 100 mSv/yr, and water-ice-rich regolith is dramatically more mass-efficient (Rostel 2020, Zhang 2022).

SPE storm shelters (~20 g/cm2, hydrogenous) are primarily a transit requirement - Mars's >=23 g/cm2 atmosphere suppresses surface SPE doses - but shelter occupancy (12-36 h/event) is a real crew-time and ops disruption.

Habitability floors for long missions: 25 m3 net habitable volume per person (Whitmire 2015 consensus) with ~5.4 m3 private quarters each (ISS's 2.1 m3 CQ is the demonstrated minimum), plus 2 contiguous rest days/week as a behavioral-health scheduling constraint.

Medical demand: ~3.4 clinical events/person-year (mostly minor, Crucian 2016), ~0.017 evac-class events/person-year (IMM); with no evacuation possible from Mars, IMM projects >10% probability of an evac-class condition per 730+ day mission and a ~1% floor on medical loss-of-crew-life for a 1195-day, 4-crew mission.
Astronauts actually sleep 6.1 h vs the 8.5 h scheduled (Barger 2014) - schedule the full 8.5 h and model the deficit as a fatigue/performance risk rather than harvestable labor.

sources.title: Life Support Baseline Values and Assumptions Document (BVAD), NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev 2
Mars' Surface Radiation Environment Measured with the Mars Science Laboratory's Curiosity Rover (Hassler et al.)
Measurements of Energetic Particle Radiation in Transit to Mars on the Mars Science Laboratory (Zeitlin et al.)
NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B and OCHMO Radiation Protection Technical Brief (600 mSv career limit)
Minimum Acceptable Net Habitable Volume for Long-Duration Exploration Missions (Whitmire et al., NASA/TM-2015-218564)
Subsurface Radiation Environment of Mars and Its Implication for Shielding Protection of Future Habitats (Röstel et al.)
Mars Radiation Environment Under Different Atmospheric and Regolith Depths (Zhang et al.)
Radiation environment for future human exploration on the surface of Mars (Guo et al. review)
Developing a Crew Time Model for Human Exploration Missions to Mars (Stromgren et al., IEEE Aerospace)
Assessment of Crew Time for Maintenance and Repair Activities for Lunar Surface Missions (Stromgren et al., IEEE Aerospace)
Prevalence of sleep deficiency and use of hypnotic drugs in astronauts (Barger et al.)
Maintenance, reliability and policies for orbital space station life support systems (Russell & Klaus)
Incidence of clinical symptoms during long-duration orbital spaceflight (Crucian et al.)
Estimating medical risk in human spaceflight (Antonsen et al.)
The Wait-less EVA Solution: Single-Person Spacecraft (Griffin, AIAA 2020-4170; ISS EVA overhead per NASA EVA-EXP-0031)
Modeling a 15-min EVA prebreathe protocol using NASA's exploration atmosphere 56.5 kPa/34% O2 (Abercromby et al.)
Solar particle event storm shelter requirements for missions beyond low Earth orbit (Townsend et al.)
International Space Station USOS Crew Quarters Development (Broyan et al., 08ICES-0222)
